SR models are enhanced by incorporating complex-valued layers for explicit frequency domain learning, as explained in the context.
Complex layers are introduced for dealing with complex-valued signals by treating the real and imaginary parts of a complex number as distinct real-valued entities.
Complex layers are specifically designed for handling complex-valued signals, as discussed in the context.
Complex-valued signals involve the real and imaginary parts of a complex number, which are treated as distinct entities within the signal processing.
Complex layers achieve complex arithmetic operations through real-valued arithmetic by treating real and imaginary parts separately.
The complex-valued input feature is convolved with a complex filter in the form of complex convolution, as detailed in the context.
The complex-valued input feature undergoes complex activation using CReLU, which applies ReLU on the real and imaginary parts separately.
The DDL method utilizes the complex layers discussed earlier to extend existing models to DDL models, as mentioned in the context.
The DDL method is applied to extend the existing EDSR model to a DDL derivative named DLL-EDSR, with specific modifications explained in the context.
In DDL-EDSR model, all ResBlocks from EDSR are replaced by DDL-RB as a modification, as depicted in the context.
DDL-RB replaces all ResBlocks in EDSR. DDL-RB adds a parallel frequency branch to the normal spatial branch in RB.
The frequency branch in DDL-RB starts with a FT.
DDL-RB employs two complex convolution layers in the frequency branch.
In the tail of EDSR, DDL-EDSR adopts two heads to output SR images and SR spectra simultaneously.
Dual-domain restrictions, LDDL = ∥I SR − I GT ∥1+λ ∥SSR − SGT ∥1, are imposed for training of DDL-EDSR.

One approach for balancing the perception-distortion trade-off is to train a PSNR-oriented network and obtain another perceptual.

The terms PSNR-oriented network and perceptual-driven network are related as they represent different approaches in the context of network models for Super-Resolution. These networks are fine-tuned and their parameters are interpolated to balance between image distortion (measured by PSNR) and perceptual quality (measured by LPIPS).

SR results are interpolated using pixel interpolation and network interpolation from two different models to achieve a perception-distortion trade-off. The process involves drawing trade-off curves representing the compromise between contradictory image distortion and perceptual quality.

The SUDF training scheme is proposed to improve the perception-distortion trade-off performance in image SR. It helps in approaching a better balance between perception and distortion in the context of Super-Resolution.

The DDL method proposed in the paper is related to image SR (Super-Resolution) as it enables the quantification of spectral uncertainty in the frequency domain. It highlights the characterization of reliability of image HF components through spectral uncertainty in the global sense.

Non-ideal input LR premises in image SR experiments demonstrate the sensitivity of spectral uncertainty against noise and adversarial conditions. The spectral uncertainty proves to be more effective in handling image HF components under such challenging input scenarios.
